name: Bhoot
description: A Bhoot (भूत) is a supernatural creature, typically the disembodied spirit of a deceased person in Hindu mythology. They are often depicted as restless and malevolent, lingering in the earthly realm due to an unnatural or untimely death, unfulfilled desires, or improper funeral rites. Bhoot are believed to inhabit desolate places, cremation grounds, or abandoned buildings, and are capable of causing mischief, illness, and even death to the living. They are often associated with darkness and are said to be most active at night. While generally considered malevolent, some Bhoot are said to be neutral or even benevolent, depending on the circumstances of their death and their attachment to the living world. The fear of Bhoot is deeply ingrained in Indian culture, leading to various rituals and practices designed to ward them off or appease them. Their existence is a reminder of the importance of proper death rites and the potential consequences of a life unfulfilled.
subtitle: Restless spirit of the deceased.
appearance: Bhoot are often described as shadowy figures, sometimes translucent or invisible. They may appear as distorted versions of their former selves, with grotesque features or injuries reflecting their cause of death. Some are described as having backward-facing feet, a common trait associated with spirits in Indian folklore. Their appearance can vary depending on the region and the specific circumstances of their existence.
habitat: Bhoot are believed to inhabit desolate and abandoned places, such as cremation grounds, graveyards, ruins, old trees, and dark corners. They are also said to be drawn to places where they experienced trauma or had strong attachments during their lifetime.
behavior: Bhoot are generally considered to be malevolent and mischievous. They may cause disturbances, such as strange noises, moving objects, or sudden temperature changes. They are also believed to possess people, causing them to act erratically or speak in strange voices. Some Bhoot seek to avenge their deaths or fulfill unfulfilled desires, while others simply enjoy tormenting the living.

keyMyths.title: Bhoot and the Haunted House
keyMyths.description: A classic tale involves a family moving into a new house only to discover it is haunted by a Bhoot. The family must then find a way to appease the spirit or drive it away, often involving a priest or other spiritual figure.

keyMyths.title: The Bhoot of the Unfulfilled Wish
keyMyths.description: This myth centers around a Bhoot who is unable to find peace because of an unfulfilled wish from their past life. Someone must help them fulfill this wish so they can finally move on.

sources.author: William Crooke
sources.work: The Popular Religion and Folklore of Northern India
sources.description: A comprehensive study of Indian folklore, including beliefs about spirits and ghosts.
sources.text: A comprehensive study of Indian folklore, including beliefs about spirits and ghosts.

sources.author: Stella Kramrisch
sources.description: While primarily about temples, it touches upon the spiritual landscape and beliefs surrounding deities and spirits.
